posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I have never been a huge fan of the 100 point scale. Even before it got bastardized by most critics trying to outscore one another to the point it became meaningless, it seemed to suggest a spurious accuracy and objectivity.

Going to the UGC tasting, I thought I would try using the 20 point scale again. It was the first scoring system; I had used, both in my native England, and the first few years after I came over. When I started writing for a magazine, they switched to the 100 point scale, and I switched with them. It took some adjustment, but used it for many years, but never particularly liked it.

At the tasting, it came back easily. And I felt that I could use it with none of the scoring hyperbole of the 100 point scale. 18 was a good score, I reserved a 19 for my favorite wine the Lalande, and my low was 13.

I am going on a tear, and say the 20 point scale is the greatest thing since the invention of fish and chips, but it is a viable scale that works for me. I would also add that only once or twice a year will I ever taste a wine that will merit 20 points. The idea that a single vintage that has double digit 100 point wines, or one 100 point wine is better than another, is clearly a sign that it is no longer working.

I’m even less enamoured by scoring. With genuine honesty I always thought the Italians had it better with scales ranging from 0-3 glasses, or 1-5 grapes, without half points either. They were less prone to score inflation, and with such a small scale, getting the top score never induced the consumer fervour for grabbing a ‘100 pointer’.

It’s a scale I’d feel competent enough to use, with the expectation of being consistently + or - 1 point.

If using a genuine 100 point scale, I could easily imagine myself tasting the same wine (duplicated) in a large line-up, and giving the glasses noticeably different points scores.

(Daniel) Rogov is the only critic I have heard of who made a point of putting such duplicates into blind line-ups, and he gave himself a + or - 2 points leeway (which I think amounted to a cheeky 4 point leeway between the glasses), in what I recall was a typically modern ‘condensed’ 100 point scale (i.e. not far off an **80-100 point scale). If his scores for the duplicate fell outside his leeway, he’d scrap the tasting notes from the session.

** I suspect pasteurised kosher wines fell below that - he was critical of the practice.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
The usefulness of a score depends entirely on whether you’re using the scale “correctly”—meaning, in the same way as everyone else. Let’s be honest: Regardless of any “official” definitions (including Parker’s own on his 100-point scale), the vast majority of critics and wine drinkers apply these scales in a similar way:

A) The 20-point scale is effectively a 16-point scale, ranging from 12 to 20 in 0.5-point increments. A score of 12 indicates a terrible wine, while 20 represents perfection.
B) The 100-point scale functions as a 15- to 20-point scale in practice, with meaningful scores typically falling between 80/85 and 100. An 80-point wine is poor, whereas a 100-point wine is outstanding.

Everyone is free to use whichever scale they prefer. There’s nothing wrong with that. But claiming one is superior to the other is a bit absurd—because, in the end, they’re essentially the same.

Another point: It’s absolutely fine to use the scales differently, such as applying the full range—everyone is free to do as they like. However, if someone uses a scale in a way that deviates from the common standard (e.g., 0 for bad, 10 (50) for good, and 20 (100) for perfect), their scores will only be meaningful to them. This undermines the score’s greatest strength: providing clear, useful information that others can easily understand.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
I think scoring is something that’s nearly impossible to do on a reproducible basis, which is why I don’t do it.

Professionally, even when scoring something much less subjective than tasting wine, like narrowing of the spinal canal or neural foramina, using only 4-6 different possible outcomes (none, mild, moderate, severe, with some people using also mild to moderate and moderate to severe) there’s dramatic difference between interpreters as well as dramatic inconsistencies within the same patient and between patients. Wine is infinitely more subjective, with all sorts of other variables like what food are you eating, the setting, who you’re with, how you’re feeling on a given day, etc.

If you’re drinking all the wines blind without food in one setting, maybe you can score them relative to each other. That being said, if you drank the exact same wines blind, the next day, I doubt the scores would be the same.

For me, I just give a narrative subjective evaluation of the wine. I have no idea, for example, how I would compare two wines drank, say, a month apart, in totally different settings. I really liked the 1998 Rousseau CSJ, and loved the 2007 Rousseau CSJ, but since I didn’t drink them at the same time, with the same food, and in the same setting, I don’t even know how I would compare them much less score them relative to each other.

posts.cooked:
MChang:

If you’re drinking all the wines blind without food in one setting, maybe you can score them relative to each other. That being said, if you drank the exact same wines blind, the next day, I doubt the scores would be the same.

Same. I rarely score wines. I am usually too lazy to write TN, but occasionally when I do, I never score the wine. I prefer to describe the overall reaction to the wine, just calling it “excellent” or “spectacular.” The reason I don’t like scoring is because it has to be tied to the moment.

Lets do a quick exercise:

Lets say you tasted a 2020 vintage wine and you gave it 95 points. You then try the same one a year later, and score it again. Repeat this process for 30 years. I highly doubt that the same 2020 wine will remain only a 95 point wine. Assuming a superior pedigree, it is safe to assume that 20 to 30 years later the wine will improve and only grow in points.

This is why I don’t like ratings. 2 people could’ve tasted the same wine at entirely different trajectories in the wines life and because of the difference in the wines age, the experience will always be different. Same with if the wine was drank alone, with food, was it decanted? For how long? etc…

That all being said, I do occasionally glance at average CT scores to get a general idea of what the public consensus is on a particular wine, but I almost never score wines unless someone puts me on the spot and requests that I do.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

I have never been a huge fan of the 100 point scale. Even before it got bastardized by most critics trying to outscore one another to the point it became meaningless, it seemed to suggest a spurious accuracy and objectivity.

Going to the UGC tasting, I thought I would try using the 20 point scale again.

Why does a 100 point scale “suggest a spurious accuracy and objectivity” but a 20 point scale, or some other number or letter scale, not? It seems like they would be identical in that regard.

I actually mildly disagree with the premise that using scoring numbers (which I rarely ever do, myself) implies objectivity. I think the point is communication.

If a taster says she thought Wine A was 19 points and Wine B was 17 points, and that helps the reader to learn a bit more about her opinion of the wines, then it is useful. It does not imply, to me, that Wine A was objectively or scientifically better than Wine B, just that she liked them to roughly those extents. Something you might not divine from her tasting notes for A and B which discuss flavors, acids, tannins, drinking windows, etc.

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.cooked:
One of the problems with the 100 point scale is the emotional baggage that comes with it from experiences in school. That’s part of the reason that it was so successful for Parker and the industry. And it helps explain a whole bunch of other behaviors that have been talked about endlessly and which I won’t elaborate here further.

I use a five point scale with +/- modifiers. I normalize around a 3 thus:

3
A good wine with respect to what it is, any shortcomings do not detract significantly, good (3-) to very good (3+) but not exciting either. Perfect for a mid-week tipple, acceptable for a weekend dinner, but opened on a special occasion or during a grand meal would likely have me looking in the cellar for another bottle to open. Glad I tried, but no reason to buy again.

4
Significantly more interesting than a 3, something to get excited about, covers the range from “this is very good and above the average” (a 4-) to “this is outstanding, thrilling, wow” (a 4+).

5
Rarely given. For wines that are among the very best of the genre, epic, the most memorable ones that need to be written about as such.

2
Wines that either are dull/uninteresting or that have flaws that significantly detract from the overall. Drinkable but disappointing in some manner.

1
Undrinkable or a complete waste of my alcohol capacity.

In our tasting groups both 100 and 20 s are used - I’d always have to calculate
I personally prefer 100 points, because I deduce from a perfection of 100p , and when too much is lacking I arrive at 90, 85, 80 or below.
20 points is not detailed enough - when I have 4 or 5 outstanding wines in front of me I end up with 18, 18+, 18.5, 19-, 19+ … and the next flight might be similar.
So 94, 95, 96, 97, 98 is much easier

posts.name: Nathan Kleba
posts.cooked:
Taking something from golf but it should apply here. Doesn’t make sense to split hairs on bulk wine.

The Doak Scale

The Doak Scale is a simple 0-10 rating system developed by Tom Doak (architect of Pacific Dunes) to give people an idea of how important a course is to visit. Doak admits that the scale is basically geared toward splitting hairs among the better courses in the world.

Definitions found in The Doak Scale

  1. A course so contrived and unnatural that it may poison your mind, one I cannot recommend under any circumstances. Reserved for courses that waste ridiculous sums of money in their construction, and probably shouldn’t have been built in the first place.
  2. A very basic golf course, with clear architectural malpractice and/or poor maintenance. Avoid even if you’re desperate for a game.
  3. A mediocre golf course with little or no architectural interest, but nothing really horrible. As my friend Dave Richards summed up: “Play it in a scramble, and drink a lot of beer.”
  4. About the level of the average golf course in the world. (Since I don’t go out of my way to see average courses, my scale is deliberately skewed to split hairs among the good, the better and the best)
  5. A modestly interesting course, with a couple of distinctive holes among the 18, or at least some scenic interest and decent golf. Also reserved for some very good courses that are much too short and narrow to provide sufficient challenge for accomplished golfers.
  6. Well above the average golf course, but the middle of my scale. A good course to choose if you’re in the vicinity and looking for a game, but don’t spend another day away from home to see it, unless your home is in Alaska.
  7. A very good course, definitely worth a game if you’re in town, but not necessarily worth a special trip to see. It shouldn’t disappoint you.
  8. An excellent course, worth checking out if you get anywhere within 100 miles. You can expect to find soundly designed, interesting holes, good course conditioning and a pretty setting, if not necessarily anything unique to the world of golf.
  9. One of the very best courses in its region (although there are more 8s in some places and none in others), and worth a special trip to see. Could have some drawbacks, but these will clearly be spelled out, and it will make up for them with something really special in addition to the generally excellent layout.
  10. An outstanding course, certainly one of the best in the world, with no weaknesses in regard to condition, length or poor holes. You should see this course sometime in your life.
  11. Nearly perfect; if you skipped even one hole, you would miss something worth seeing. If you haven’t seen all the courses in this category, you don’t know how good golf architecture can get. Call your travel agent immediately.

I have never been a huge fan of the 100 point scale. Even before it got bastardized by most critics trying to outscore one another to the point it became meaningless, it seemed to suggest a spurious accuracy and objectivity.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Any of the scoring systems are fine on paper. To me, the 100 point scale has one advantage over the others that, in my opinion, trumps the other considerations.

Continuing with my theme that scores are about communication, when you use the 100 point scale, most wine people know what you mean. It has the advantage of the widest familiarity.

All the criticisms of the 100 point scale and how it gets used are entirely valid, but at the end of the day, if I’m right that the real purpose is communication, I think it is a better choice than the others because it is just more broadly successful for communication.

You write a post about trying a flight of four 2006 Bordeaux, and you write notes about them, and you score them 84, 89, 92 and 95, everyone on here who reads it knows roughly what you mean. They can pick at it and find all the reasons it isn’t perfect, but in reality, they more or less know what you mean.

Again, I hardly ever use any kind of scoring myself, but as someone reading other notes, I’d generally rather read someone using the 100 point scale in roughly the mainstream way it gets used. That is totally just me, and I understand all the valid reasons people on here feel otherwise.

The origins of the UC Davis 20 pt scale: Back in the '40-'50’s, they (who they??) did research at Davis that indicated that 20 points was the maximum gradations of quality a taster could discern. Hence the evolution of the UCDavis 20 pt scale. This scale was then used in their subsequent studies in quality in wine and was widely used by wine critics of that time. Anyone remember Jerry Mead & Robert Lawrence Balzer??
However a UC Davis score correlated poorly w/ how much you liked the wine. Hence the evolution of Parker’s 100 pt hedonistic scale. It give a false degree of precision that is not warranted.

Actually, if you want to know how good a wine is, you simply read the Bloody Pulpit. That’s a system that is taking the World by storm. Pfaw on numerical scores.
Tom

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Somewhere along the way I switched from scoring about 20% to 30% of the wines for which I wrote a TN to scoring less than 5% (last year). I’ve always used a 100 point scale, and I like the 100 point scale when used by any given taster in a consistent manner. I have a very strict way of going about scoring any wine I choose to score — when you see me break-down my score into components. Instead of going through this process, which is relatively arduous, I often instead offer-up a “gut impression score” – usually a two-to-four point range – that is meant to approximate where I feel l would score the wine if I went through my strict scoring method. My “gut impression score” reflects me a couple times quickly running through my scoring system in my head — the first quick run-through I choose to be miserly, and the second quick run-through I’m as generous as I can be, and from that my “gut impression score” range is born. Oftentimes, I don’t even give a gut impression score anymore.

Why this change over time? Because I don’t gain nearly as much from scoring wines now as I did in the past. Sometime in the past few years, I realized that my strict-scoring-method scores were almost always landing exactly on, or one point away from, my Gut Impression score, and that’s when I realized there was no longer any sort of regular advantage to be gained by scoring in the strict/arduous manner. … My TNs, first and foremost, have always been for my own reference. So, with that in mind, if strict scoring method scores are no longer perceived to be meaningfully more helpful to me than Gut Impression scores, then why do them? And that’s why I usually don’t. But, every once in a while, for fun, I’ll do the involved scoring thing.

I appreciate others who do score wines as part of their TNs, if they’re consistent and thoughtful in how they approach their scoring (whatever their approach may be, short of an approach that includes little thought). But I also do not hold it against anyone who chooses not to score; at the end of the day, someone who reviews a wine using only words has written a far more helpful note than somone who publishes a score only.

WOW! This basically correlates to why nearly all CT scores are between 80-100. The crowd realized 20 points is all they can do. And they anchored to the top, i.e. 80-100. Very cool.

Here’s the problem: The scale is being applied to wines mostly by critics or wine geeks who are drinking the top 20% of wines by quality. One would expect then, that the vast majority of the wines rated would fall between an 80 and a 100, and only statistical outliers would fall below the 80 point threshold. Thus, a 79 is indicative not just of a poor quality wine, but of a startlingly poor quality of wine relative to expectation. The full 100 point scale is reserved for Thunderbucket tastings of $4.99 clearance chardonnays that have been sitting on the front windo rack of your wine store, that backyard grape wine your Italian uncle is proud of making every year, and wines like Meomi.

At least a 100 point scale takes that into account. Sure, 1-79 are almost never used, but Wine Spectator isn’t going out of its way to taste Cisco Red or Boones Farm Country Kwencher.

When you use a 20 point scale, where do the bottom 80% bottles of wine fall? 1-16? The 20 point scale reserves palty 12s for liquid turds, but suggests a 15 is a really solid bordeaux that, on a more global scale, would easily fall into the top 15-20% of wines produced. Is it really a 15? Is 15/20 the same as 75/100? Shouldn’t it be? It isn’t though.

And the same people who are like, I preferred this wine ever so slightly more than this one, so its a 16 here and a 15 for that one…are they really doing anything at all differently than saying hey, using an unanchored arbitrary scale based on my own bullshit subjective assessment of quality based on my body chemistry today–affected by diet and environment–I like this one just “one point more” than that one?

Is your 20 point scale based on variety? Is a Bojo that’s a 19 as good as a GC Burg that’s a 19 or is that by category? What points are reserved for the truly special? Can a truly special pinot grigio challenge the greatest montrachet? How does the scale address that issue?

None of it matters. It’s just scaling. It’s ranking wines by order of preference within an arbitraty metric. 20, 40, 100 points, who gives a shit. It really doesn’t matter. What matters is knowing a critic’s scale and matching preferences. Sucklenuts could be usefull if you have a similar palate and know a 99 is probably better for you than a 96. Alfert knows a Leve 99 is probably worse for him than a Leve 91 in a ripe vintage.

I could develop a scale that says okay, Donkey = my absolute top wines of all time. Those show stopping monsters that send you into core memory creation mode. Then below that are wines that are just amazing, if not necessarily emotionally moving = Leopard. And so on. I could chose 30 animals, 20, 50, 20 but I could say they are wearing a crown so you know something is a top tier leopard, better than other leopards, but not quite a Donkey. All that matters is the information I’m conveying. Jancis can’t even help herself with her 20 point scale. Add half points and it’s a 40 point scale. Except still not really because the bottom half isn’t used. But again, it doesn’t matter. It’s just a method through which to relay relative preference.

I don’t like scoring wines, but in my head I am probably using the 4 point system of letter grades: A, B, C, D even though I am not writing them down. I suppose that, to me, they mean:
A: I really like this. Yum. I would like to have some and drink it again.
B: This is good if it is cheap enough to be a casual house wine. Otherwise I don’t care to search it out.
C: it’s OK, average, but not worth my time. Life is too short.
D: Sucks.

In one group where we are supposed to rank the wines, I have been forced to add pluses and minuses (so I can try to pretend that there is some order of preference among 9-10 wines), which converts it to a 10 point scale (a D is a D, and there is no D+ or D-).

I do not judge individual components of color, nose, etc.
Having spent many years getting letter grades in school, it is easier and more natural to me than assigning numbers.

posts.name: Jacob Goldman
posts.cooked: I’m sorry, but this is nonsensical. There is no a priori reason to believe the distribution of scores would follow a (truncated given it is capped below and above) bell curve shape. What is likely, however, is that the distribution of the estimated arithmetic mean will converge towards a bell curve shape if we keep increasing the number of scores (the Lindeberg-Levy central limit theorem would apply here).

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Seems to me that all modes of scoring, ranking, or evaluating a wine are inherently flawed, as they are so inherently subjective to the reviewer. Many of the reasons have already been noted above. Now that said, I’m always amused by the angst over scoring a wine, as that to me is such a basic mode of communicating one’s pleasure or displeasure, and to what degree, about the wine itself. And for that purpose, I have always found the 100-point scale, inherently intuitive, it basically mirrors the grading system most of us in the United States grew up under, and it allows for more granularity in scoring than most of the other compressed systems. At this point, it’s pretty much the universal standard and will not go away. Coupled with a detailed tasting note - look at how Otto and Keith L review wines so coherently - is my method of choice, and the one I migrate toward reading most on this site.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Rahsaan_M:
MChang:

I just think the issue with using points to score is that they’re usually neither internally consistent (within a given session) or externally consistent (between sessions).

Well bottles of wine are living entities and they are not consistent across sessions, so why should our response be consistent. And of course we are not consistent either, as all sorts of things vary and affect our experience.

I’m hardly a defender of points as I never use them or read them. But all of these issues exist with TNs as well. It’s all just fleeting impressions. I thought we knew that.

But it’s why I find i so hilarious when people talk about “100-point” wines as if that were somehow a property of every bottle of that wine!

So given this, let me add caveats with any scoring system. Perhaps there should be an asterisk and footnotes.

  1. They are only useful as a single data point for one taster at the time he was tasting or drinking the wine. My 20 point system definitely works for me, but I make no claim whatsoever that it is anything except my very subjective tasting of one or more wines on one particular day. I may extrapolate, but that is because I have had a long acquaintance with me the taster.

  2. The following may have influenced a score.

(a) What he had for lunch,
(b) What was the the weather like ( high or low pressure)
(c) How long the bottle has been opened,
(d) Was the wine poured from the top, middle or bottom. Were older wines decanted, and if so, how long before being tasted.
(e) Inherent bias. This would include alcohol tolerance, and whether there was a preference for traditional or more modern wines.
(f) Commercial pressures, does the taster have a financial motive to score the wine high. This would include access, shelf talkers etc
(g) Taster’s mood. Was he stuck in traffic, did he just have a fight with spouse etc?
(h) how experienced is the taster.
(g) was the tasting open or blind
(h) What other wines were tasted at the same time.
(i) How long did he/she spend tasting, Was it pour, taste, discard, or a longer examination of the wine.

Etc etc

I know this is a thing to say in these discussions, but I think everyone, you included, gets some nonzero amount of understanding of what the taster’s subjective opinion was from the difference between:

“Notes of tobacco, menthol and leather, dark fruits which are reticent at this stage, significant tannins which suggest patience with the wine. Oak is present but may integrate better with time. 88 points.”

“Notes of tobacco, menthol and leather, dark fruits which are reticent at this stage, significant tannins which suggest patience with the wine. Oak is present but may integrate better with time. 94 points.”

The goal is to communicate, in words, the taster’s perception of the wine. And that is very difficult to do, so I say anything that helps even slightly in doing so, go right ahead and use it, and you don’t deserve scorn for the imperfections about it.

If the person uses something imprecise (“white flowers”), literally incorrect (“graphite”), goofy (“sea fossils”), numerical (“94 points”), or un-PC within reason (“feminine”), yet some readers have a sense of what the writer means, then I’m totally fine with it.

I (way too often) say in these threads say, don’t get too critical, demanding or mean about amateur tasting notes. It will just mean fewer and fewer people will write them and share about wine, and that isn’t for the better overall.

Joseph_Grassa:

I agree with most of your opinion, but it feels more like you have a problem with the interpretation of ratings more so than the ratings themselves. Ratings are way too important to many, and since then, become too unimportant to others imo. A rating is merely a way of offering more quantifiable information about something that is really impossible to quantify.

If someone offered me a glass of wine A or wine B, but not of both, and the only information I have about the wine is that Monica Larner gave A 96pts, and B 88pts, I would without hesitation take the glass of wine A. If it were 97pts for wine B, I would go with wine B. Of course I would prefer more information, but there are millions of wines, and it becomes more and more challenging to process the info.

I am actually pro rating because ratings do convey useful information by providing a ranking system for wines. I’m not at all opposed to ratings. My point is that discourse on whether a 5 star, 20 point, or 100 point system is superior is generally not worthwhile because the systems are each similarly and inherently and in my opinion unavoidably flawed. I mean, I drink a ton of champagne and love a good BdB and generally prefer them to BdN. I typically don’t favor rose. If I’m reviewing ratings from someone who greatly prefers a BdN to a BdB, and who just dies for Rose, even the most diligent application of a 100 point scale won’t necessarily result in a hierarchy of rankings I agree with. I mean some people actually love the taste of asparagus or, say, fermented herring. How could you ever trust their ranking to be consistent with how you would rank something as a man of taste?

Your comments about automatically choosing wine a wine Larner gave a 96 over one she gave an 88 suggests you have an aligned palate with Larner. If my pal Bill from the country club who only drinks Hundred Acre and thinks white wines are for pussies says wine A is a 96 and wine B is an 88, I’m seriously leaning wine B.

The trouble with average consumer focused wine ratings is that an unscrupulous critic like, say for the sake of a hypothetical, Suckling knows that retailers want to sell wines to consumers. If he gives wine A a 95 and Larner gives the wine a 91, retailers all over the world will put a Suckling shelf talker in their store to sell the wine. Knowing this, wineries will clamor to have Suckling try their wines, make videos, find sources of revenue, etc. so that they can get gaudy shelf talkers to move more inventory at higher margins. The hundred point scale is especially effective for this type of campaigning because 100 is higher than 20, and 98 looks better than 19 or 19.5. In every country, regardless of education/grading background. Everyone would prefer 98 Euro in their wallet to 19 Euro. The result is a consumer drive race to score inflation.

I’m guilty. I bought a 2019 Brunello that received a 100 point score from 3 different critics (and is under $100). I don’t even love Brunello, but everyone is affected by marketing, and the allure of wine perfection attested to by three THREE different competing critics made my weeny wiggle.

posts.name: Jeff Leve

This board lives in its own, unique anti-critic world. So, anything that changes the status quo of critics works.

The 100 Pt scale has been almost universally adopted because consumers prefer it. It’s not likely to change because it’s easy to understand.

Any critic, writer, reviewer, etc., that doesn’t adopt it is not going to be accepted. A few old-timers from the UK prefer 20pts, but with .5s, you have 40 pts, making it just about100 pts cut in half. So what’s new here ? It’s only smoke and mirrors.

At the end of the day, if you like, or don’t like any critic, there’s a reason. So follow them, or not. Scores are not awarded in a vacuum. It’s the reason behind the score that counts.

Are scores too high today? For some wines, yes. I find that’s usually for wines in the middle from tasters seeking to get quoted. The wines at the top end IMO, at least for the ones I’ve tasted, deserve high marks. Does it really matter if it’s 98, 99 or 100? For the consumer, not really. It’s the reason that counts.

No, the scale is not only 20 Pts. But the wines most tasters review are among the best in their region, they’re not the lower end wines most of you would never buy.

For example, so far, I’ve tasted close to 500 2022 BDX in bottle, with 250-300 to go. Perhaps 10 are in the very low 80s, with numerous, moderate wines scoring in the mid 80s. The remainder of the wines are simply good, to very good, to great wines from a top vintage! They deserve very high marks.

At the end of the day, this board represents a tiny, vocal fraction of the wine world. Wineries, merchants and most importantly consumers like, use, and understand the 100 Pt. Scale.

And obviously, what some of us really rely on from the critics is access, especially early access, to wines that we may not be able to taste before we buy. And context, some critics have great context to their reviews given them many years they may have been following a particular

You also need to add consistency. When I find liquid sex, (I deserve royalties for creating that) I should always find that when it’s there. And when I don’t like specific characteristics, I should be reasonably resolute and not champion those wines.

Sadly, experience is a euphemism for getting old. It’s going to be harder for today’s tasters to gain the same level of experience the previous generation had. But that’s another topic worthy its own discussion.

Access as you pointed out is one of the best reasons to read a taster. For example, the topic of 2016 Latour just came up. In April, it will be my 3rd or 4th tasting of the wine. Yet, they’re are numerous opinions on the quality along with drinkability from folks that have never tasted the wine! And that’s just one example.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Ian_Sutton:

(Daniel) Rogov is the only critic I have heard of who made a point of putting such duplicates into blind line-ups, and he gave himself a + or - 2 points leeway (which I think amounted to a cheeky 4 point leeway between the glasses), in what I recall was a typically modern ‘condensed’ 100 point scale (i.e. not far off an **80-100 point scale). If his scores for the duplicate fell outside his leeway, he’d scrap the tasting notes from the session.

I’ve understood that the Swedish alcohol monopoly does this when assessing the wines for their tenders - and there can be consequences if one is not consistent with their assessments!

Anyways, I haven’t been to tastings where someone would put duplicates into lineups, but many times I’ve tasted a wine blind and then tasting it again (blind) some months or maybe a year apart - and given the wine either the exact same score as before, or within one or two points. Also with more or less the exact same descriptors in the TN.

I might not be able to identify all the imaginable wines blind nor am I consistent enough to score every wine the exact same way all the time, but I’ve seen it happen more than enough times to consider myself a pretty reliable taster/assessor of wine.

Of course that’s what scores do. Look, when I score a wine highly, it’s because I want consumers to buy it, because it’s a great wine! That’s the point of points.

Consumers make their own choice once they taste a wine. Regardless of a high or low score, at the end of the day, all that matters is personal taste.

MChang:

The problem is that the points aren’t consistent even for the same “reviewer” so meaningless. They’d do better just saying what they actually think in the note.

Inconsistent or imperfect does not equal meaningless. Saying mineral, tobacco, olive, tannic, acidic or anything else is also inconsistent and imperfect.

I know you’re not one to ever admit or concede anything, but anyone who read our last exchange can see that you did, in fact, discern the meaning conveyed by the points.

If we disallow imperfection in tasting notes, there will be no tasting notes. That’s why I waste time pushing back against the scorn in these threads, I’d rather there be more communication than less in the world of wine.

Sure, I did, but thought it could be stated better with words rather than a number.

Maybe I’ll restate my statement as giving a numeric score is superfluous when done so along with a well-written tasting note. I think most people expect you to say whether or not you like the wine when you write a note.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Paul_Bruck:
David_Bu3ker:

I am wondering what “new numbers” exist from 0-100.

It’s not like we’re scoring in hexadecimal or using imaginary numbers.

The 100-p-scale is in fact a 50-100 p-scale … and usually 70 to100 p, 70p being a wine on the edge of drinkable (no major flaws, no qualities).
The 20-p-scale is actually 10 to 20 points,
85p is equal to 15 points which both I’d call “good”

Sure everybody can use his own scale personally, but the purpose of communication is flawed then …

I use the Suckling 4 point scale.
